---
title: "Why Do Some Songs Blow Up on Spotify While Most Are Never Heard?"
sidebar: false
toc: false
page-layout: article
---

<!-- Story skeleton — Angelina, April 2026 -->
<!-- Chart placeholders marked for Cécile. -->

## Opening

<!-- Hook the reader. Set up the central question: why do some songs reach people and most don't?
     No data yet — just pull them in. -->

---

## Section 1 — How popular are most songs, really? {#sec-popularity}

<!-- What goes here:
     - The surprising reality of how skewed popularity is (most songs near zero)
     - Sets up the "so what separates the rare ones?" question
     - Should feel like the hook that makes the reader want to keep scrolling -->

*[Chart 1 — Popularity distribution]*

---

## Section 2 — Which features are linked to popularity? {#sec-correlations}

<!-- What goes here:
     - Define the 9 audio features in plain language (one sentence each, first use)
     - The key finding: correlations exist but are all weak — no single feature predicts popularity
     - Strongest signal: instrumentalness (vocals help), then loudness and danceability
     - Also: the features predict *each other* more than popularity -->

*[Chart 2a — Feature-to-popularity correlations]*

*[Chart 2b — Feature correlation matrix]*

---

## Section 3 — How do genres differ from each other? {#sec-genres}

<!-- What goes here:
     - Show how differently genres "sound" across the 9 features
     - Classical and metal are at opposite extremes; pop and rock cluster in the middle
     - Hip-hop leads on danceability; jazz and classical are calmest
     - Reader can select genres to compare (interactive) -->

*[Chart 3 — Genre radar chart with genre selector]*

---

## Section 4 — What does a hit actually sound like? {#sec-hits}

<!-- What goes here:
     - Compare top 10% vs bottom 10% in popularity
     - Hits tend to be louder, more danceable, more energetic, less acoustic, less instrumental
     - Valence (happy/sad) makes almost no difference — important finding
     - IMPORTANT: be clear this is a pattern, not a formula. Correlation ≠ causation. -->

*[Chart 4 — Hits vs non-hits comparison]*

---

## Section 5 — Where do genres sit on a happy/energetic map? {#sec-moodmap}

<!-- What goes here:
     - Map all 113 genres by valence (X: sad↔happy) and energy (Y: calm↔intense)
     - Key finding: most music is energetic regardless of mood — the calm half is small
     - Locate the 6 focus genres on the map
     - This is the wrap-up section — ties everything together -->

*[Chart 5 — Genre mood map]*

---

## Closing

<!-- What goes here:
     - Land the honest conclusion: no audio feature reliably predicts popularity
     - Acknowledge what the data can't explain (timing, luck, discovery, marketing)
     - End on something the reader takes with them -->
